title: React Fundamentals
summary: JSX, components, props, and the virtual DOM mental model — how React figures out what to paint on screen.

questions.prompt: JSX is valid JavaScript that browsers execute directly:
choices: True — modern browsers support JSX natively
False — JSX is transformed to React.createElement() calls by the build tool before reaching the browser
True — only for Chrome
False — JSX is converted to HTML strings
questions.explanation:
Browsers have no knowledge of JSX. Vite/Babel transforms `

Hello

` to `React.createElement('h1', null, 'Hello')` before bundling. The browser only ever sees JavaScript.

questions.prompt: What renders when count = 0?
choices: Nothing — falsy short-circuits
Items: 0
The text '0'
An empty <div/>
questions.explanation: `count && …` when count=0: `0` is falsy so the right side is not evaluated. JSX renders the left side value `0` as the text character '0'. Use `count > 0 &&` to fix this.

questions.prompt: Props in React are:
choices: Mutable state managed by the child component
Read-only data passed from parent to child — the child must never mutate them
Shared global state between siblings
Only available in class components
questions.explanation: Props flow down. Read-only enforced by convention (and TypeScript readonly). Mutation breaks the one-way data flow contract React relies on for predictable rendering.

questions.prompt: Why should list items use a stable, unique id as the React key instead of array index?
choices: Index keys cause a console warning only
With index keys, React cannot match elements correctly on reorder/insert — DOM nodes get wrong state and animations break
Index keys are slower to compute
React ignores index keys entirely
questions.explanation: Keys are React's identity signal. If item B moves from index 1 to index 0, an index key tells React 'index 0 changed' — it reuses the DOM node from the old index 0 (item A). Stable IDs ensure each item keeps its correct DOM node.

questions.prompt: Which attribute name is correct in JSX?
choices: Option A — JSX uses HTML attribute names
Option B — JSX uses camelCase DOM property names
Both are valid
Neither — use style prop instead
questions.explanation: JSX maps to DOM properties, not HTML attributes. `class` → `className` (class is a reserved JS word). `for` → `htmlFor` (for is a reserved JS word). HTML attributes work in actual HTML files, not JSX.

questions.prompt: The Virtual DOM is:
choices: A faster version of the browser DOM built into Chrome
A plain JavaScript object tree that React builds in memory, diffs against the previous render, and uses to compute minimal DOM operations
The shadow DOM API
A cached copy of the real DOM stored in localStorage
questions.explanation: The virtual DOM is just plain JavaScript objects — lightweight and fast to create. React diffs (compares) old and new vDOM trees to figure out the minimum set of real DOM changes needed.

questions.prompt: Which are valid JSX rules?
choices: Must return exactly one root element (or use a Fragment)
Self-closing tags must be explicitly closed:
Attribute values that are strings must use {{ double braces }}
JavaScript expressions go inside { single curly braces }
questions.explanation: String attribute values use standard quotes: `className="title"`. Double braces `{{ }}` are only for inline style objects: `style={{ color: 'red' }}`. All other options are correct JSX rules.

questions.prompt: What is the TypeScript type of the `onClick` prop here?
choices: Function
() => void | undefined
A function that takes no arguments and returns nothing, optionally provided
EventHandler
questions.explanation: The `?` makes the prop optional (can be undefined). `() => void` means a function with no parameters that returns nothing (void). Together: the prop may or may not be passed, and if passed must match that signature.

questions.prompt: Two sibling JSX elements must be wrapped in a parent. To avoid adding an extra DOM node, use:
choices: <div>
React.Fragment or the <> shorthand
React.StrictMode
A comment node
questions.explanation: `<React.Fragment>` or `<>…</>` groups children without emitting a DOM element. Using `<div>` adds an extra DOM node that may break CSS layouts.

questions.prompt: When does React call a function component again (re-render)?
choices: On every browser tick
When its props or internal state changes, or when an ancestor re-renders
Only when the user interacts
Once — on initial mount only
questions.explanation: React re-renders a component when: its own state changes, its props change, or any ancestor re-renders (unless memoised). This is why managing what triggers re-renders is central to React performance.

questions.prompt: What does this component return when `show` is false?
choices: Nothing — the component throws
— an empty div with no children
null
The component is unmounted
questions.explanation: `null` is a valid JSX return value meaning 'render nothing here'. The outer `<div>` still renders. The component is not unmounted — it stays in the tree but its child is absent.

questions.prompt: Data flow in React is:
choices: Bidirectional — child can push data back up via props
Unidirectional — data flows down via props; events flow up via callback props
Any direction via the Context API
Managed entirely by the browser event system
questions.explanation: React enforces one-way data flow: parent passes data down as props. Children communicate upward by calling callback functions (also passed as props). Context is a way to skip intermediaries but still flows down.

questions.prompt: Identify the bug:
choices: No bug — count is a local variable in the function scope
count++ would be better
Mutating a prop parameter — TypeScript should flag this; the parent's state is not actually changed, but this pattern is confusing and wrong
The return statement is missing parentheses
questions.explanation: Props are conceptually read-only. While JavaScript lets you reassign a parameter, it doesn't change the parent's value. TypeScript with `readonly` would error. The intent should be `const display = count + 1` — a derived value, not mutation.

questions.prompt: What happens to a React component when it is 'unmounted'?
choices: Its useState values are preserved for next mount
It is removed from the virtual DOM and real DOM; useState values are lost
It becomes invisible but stays in memory
Only its children are removed
questions.explanation: Unmounting destroys the component instance. All state is lost. Cleanup functions from useEffect run. On the next mount the component starts fresh with initial state.

questions.prompt: Which of these cause a React component to re-render?
choices: Parent component re-renders
A ref value changes
Component's useState setter is called
A local variable inside the component changes
questions.explanation: Ref changes do NOT trigger re-renders (that is the point of refs). Local variable changes are lost on each render and do not trigger them either. Parent re-render and state updates do.

questions.prompt: React's reconciler compares virtual DOM trees using which primary heuristic?
choices: Deep equality of all props recursively
Element type + key: same type means update, different type means unmount+remount
Component display name
DOM node reference equality
questions.explanation: React first checks if the element type is the same. If `<div>` becomes `<span>`, React unmounts the div and mounts a new span (expensive). If type is same, it updates props in place. Keys identify items within sibling lists.

questions.prompt: What does React render for the key='0' vs key={item.id} difference when you insert an item at start?
choices: React creates 1 new DOM node (Charlie) and keeps Alice and Bob
React creates 3 new DOM nodes — sees all indices changed
React updates all 3 text nodes — same number of ops as stable keys
React moves DOM nodes without recreating them
questions.explanation: With index keys: index 0 changed Alice→Charlie, index 1 changed Bob→Alice, index 2 is new Bob. React updates all 3 text content. With id keys: only 1 new node (Charlie) is created; Alice and Bob are reused. Same op count here BUT state (input focus, checked state) would be wrong.

questions.prompt: A component that returns `null` is:
choices: Mounted but renders nothing to the DOM
Unmounted from the tree
An error — components must return JSX
A portal
questions.explanation: Returning `null` is perfectly valid. The component stays in the virtual DOM tree (so state is preserved, effects run) but contributes nothing to the real DOM. Unmounting requires the parent to stop rendering the component entirely.

questions.prompt: The JSX expression `<MyComponent />` compiles to:
choices: document.createElement('MyComponent')
React.createElement(MyComponent, null)
new MyComponent(null)
ReactDOM.render(MyComponent)
questions.explanation: Lowercase tags (`<div>`) compile to `createElement('div', …)` (string). Capitalized tags (`<MyComponent>`) compile to `createElement(MyComponent, …)` (function reference). This is how React distinguishes HTML elements from custom components.

questions.prompt: You have a list component receiving an array prop. The parent re-renders with the same array contents but a new array reference. The child:
choices: Does not re-render — React does deep equality on arrays
Re-renders — React does shallow (reference) equality on props by default
Re-renders only if the array length changed
Throws an error — arrays cannot be props
questions.explanation: React.memo and React.PureComponent use shallow equality (===). A new array `[]` !== the previous `[]` even with the same contents. Without memoisation, the child re-renders. This is why `useMemo` / stable references matter.

flashcards.back: Lightweight JS object tree. React diffs old vs new vDOM → computes minimal real DOM operations. Never write to the DOM directly.
`React.createElement(type, props, ...children)`. Lowercase = HTML element string. Uppercase = component function reference.
Read-only. Data flows down, events flow up via callback props. Never mutate props — derive new values instead.
Use stable, unique IDs from data. Never array index. Wrong keys → wrong DOM reuse → state bugs on list reorder.
Renders the text '0' — falsy value propagates to DOM. Fix: `count > 0 && <X />` or `Boolean(count) && <X />`.
Groups siblings without adding a DOM node. Use over <div> when a wrapper would break CSS or add semantically meaningless nesting.
1) Own state changes. 2) Props change (shallow ref check). 3) Ancestor re-renders. Refs do NOT trigger re-renders.
Valid — component stays mounted (state preserved), but renders nothing to DOM. Different from unmounting, which destroys state.

project.brief: Design the component tree for a 'Contact List' feature: a search box, a list of contact cards, and a detail panel that appears when a card is clicked. 1) Draw the component tree with component names and props shown on each edge. 2) Identify which component owns the search query state and which owns the selected contact state — justify why. 3) Show how the search query flows down to filter the list, without the ContactCard knowing about the query. 4) Identify two places where wrong keys would cause bugs and explain what the bug would be. 5) Which props in your design should be optional? Why?

checklist.text: Component tree drawn with at least 4 components and labelled props on edges
State ownership justified — explains lifting state to the right level
Filtering logic stays in parent — ContactCard only receives filtered data
Two concrete key-bug scenarios identified with specific effects
Optional props identified with reasoning (onClick on a display-only card, etc.)

hints: State should live in the closest common ancestor of all components that need it.
ContactCard should not know about the search query — it just renders data. The parent filters before passing.
Key bug scenario 1: list reorder with index keys — focused input jumps to wrong item.
Key bug scenario 2: removing first item with index keys — all items shift, React reuses wrong DOM nodes.
